title: Installation Guide for XQTL2.Xplore
text: installation, verify everything works:
code: # Load the package library(XQTL2.Xplore)  # Check what's available ls("package:XQTL2.Xplore")  # Check available data data(package = "XQTL2.Xplore")  # Load and examine example data data(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan) data(zinc_hanson_means)  # Quick test - should work without errors head(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan) dim(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan)

title: Installation Guide for XQTL2.Xplore
text: basic installation works, test core features: Success indicators: - ✅ Package loads without errors - ✅ Data loads successfully - ✅ Zoom function works shows plot - ✅ 5-panel plot generates without errors
code: # Load all required data data(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan) data(zinc_hanson_means) data(dm6.ncbiRefSeq.genes) data(dm6.variants)  # Test peak detection and zooming out <- XQTL_zoom(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan, "chr3R", 18000000, 20000000, 3, 3)  # View the zoomed region print(out$plot)  # Test the main plotting function XQTL_5panel_plot(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan, zinc_hanson_means,                   dm6.variants, dm6.ncbiRefSeq.genes,                   out$chr, out$start, out$stop)

title: Using XQTL2.Xplore
text: XQTL2.Xplore provides tools visualizing XQTL (X-chromosome Quantitative Trait Locus) data. vignette demonstrates use package’s main functions.

title: Using XQTL2.Xplore
text: package includes several pre-processed data objects:

title: Using XQTL2.Xplore
text: dm6.ncbiRefSeq.genes: Processed gene annotations dm6 reference genome dm6.variants: Processed variant data multiple VCF files

title: Using XQTL2.Xplore
text: zinc_hanson_pseudoscan: QTL scan results ZINC Hanson experiment zinc_hanson_means: Founder frequency data ZINC Hanson experiment

title: Using XQTL2.Xplore
text: data objects can updated independently using functions data-raw directory: Note: VCF processing computationally intensive, run create_variants_object.R necessary.
code: # To update the genes data (from GTF file) source("XQTL2.Xplore/data-raw/create_genes_object.R") devtools::document() devtools::install() data(dm6.ncbiRefSeq.genes)  # To update the variants data (from VCF files) source("data-raw/create_variants_object.R") devtools::document() devtools::install() data(dm6.variants)  # To update example datasets source("data-raw/create_example_data.R") devtools::document() devtools::install() data(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan) data(zinc_hanson_means)

title: Using XQTL2.Xplore
code: library(XQTL2.Xplore)  # Load reference data data(dm6.ncbiRefSeq.genes) data(dm6.variants)  # Load example datasets data(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan) data(zinc_hanson_means)

title: Using XQTL2.Xplore
text: data, can load using standard R functions:
code: # Load your own QTL scan results my_qtl_data <- read.table("my_pseudoscan.txt", header = TRUE)  # Load your own frequency data my_freq_data <- read.table("my_meansBySample.txt", header = TRUE)

title: Using XQTL2.Xplore
code: # Using example data XQTL_Manhattan_5panel(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan)  # Using your own data XQTL_Manhattan_5panel(my_qtl_data)  # Single Manhattan plot XQTL_Manhattan(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan)

title: Using XQTL2.Xplore
code: # Using example data A1 = XQTL_region(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan, "chr3R", 13000000, 15000000, "Wald_log10p") A2 = XQTL_change_average(zinc_hanson_means, "chr3R", 13000000, 15000000) A3 = XQTL_genes(dm6.ncbiRefSeq.genes, "chr3R", 13000000, 15000000)  # Using your own data A1 = XQTL_region(my_qtl_data, "chr3R", 13000000, 15000000, "Wald_log10p") A2 = XQTL_change_average(my_freq_data, "chr3R", 13000000, 15000000) A3 = XQTL_genes(dm6.ncbiRefSeq.genes, "chr3R", 13000000, 15000000)

title: Using XQTL2.Xplore
code: # Using example data out = XQTL_zoom(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan, "chr3R", 13000000, 15000000, left_drop = 3, right_drop = 3)  # Using your own data out = XQTL_zoom(my_qtl_data, "chr3R", 13000000, 15000000, left_drop = 3, right_drop = 3)

title: Using XQTL2.Xplore
text: XQTL2.Xplore provides wide range visualization tools. ’s comprehensive overview:

previous_headings: Basic Usage > Comprehensive Plotting Functions Showcase
title: Using XQTL2.Xplore
code: # 5-panel Manhattan plot (recommended for high-resolution analysis) XQTL_Manhattan_5panel(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan, cM = FALSE)  # Traditional Manhattan plot with custom color schemes XQTL_Manhattan(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan, cM = FALSE, color_scheme = "UCI") XQTL_Manhattan(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan, cM = TRUE, color_scheme = "Stanford")

previous_headings: Basic Usage > Comprehensive Plotting Functions Showcase
title: Using XQTL2.Xplore
code: # QTL association in a specific region XQTL_region(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan, "chr3R", 18000000, 20000000, "Wald_log10p")  # Frequency changes between treatment and control XQTL_change_average(zinc_hanson_means, "chr3R", 18000000, 20000000) XQTL_change_average(zinc_hanson_means, "chr3R", 18000000, 20000000, plotSelection = TRUE)  # Frequency changes by replicate XQTL_change_byRep(zinc_hanson_means, "chr3R", 18000000, 20000000)

previous_headings: Basic Usage > Comprehensive Plotting Functions Showcase
title: Using XQTL2.Xplore
code: # Gene annotations in a region XQTL_genes(dm6.ncbiRefSeq.genes, "chr3R", 18000000, 20000000)  # Variant analysis across founders XQTL_variantsByFounder(dm6.variants, "chr3R", 18000000, 20000000, zinc_hanson_means)  # Structural variants by size XQTL_SVBySize(dm6.variants, "chr3R", 18000000, 20000000, zinc_hanson_means)

previous_headings: Basic Usage > Comprehensive Plotting Functions Showcase
title: Using XQTL2.Xplore
code: # Complete 5-panel visualization XQTL_5panel_plot(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan, zinc_hanson_means,                   dm6.variants, dm6.ncbiRefSeq.genes,                   "chr3R", 18000000, 20000000)  # Custom multi-panel layouts using patchwork library(patchwork) A1 <- XQTL_region(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan, "chr3R", 18000000, 20000000, "Wald_log10p") A2 <- XQTL_change_average(zinc_hanson_means, "chr3R", 18000000, 20000000) A3 <- XQTL_genes(dm6.ncbiRefSeq.genes, "chr3R", 18000000, 20000000) A4 <- XQTL_variantsByFounder(dm6.variants, "chr3R", 18000000, 20000000, zinc_hanson_means)  # Custom layout (A1 / A2) | (A3 / A4)

title: Using XQTL2.Xplore
text: plotting functions use ggplot2 can customized using standard ggplot2 syntax:
code: # Example: Customizing a Manhattan plot p = XQTL_Manhattan(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan) p + theme_minimal() +      labs(title = "My Custom Title")

title: Using XQTL2.Xplore
text: large genomic regions, consider using zoom function identify interesting peaks:
code: # First find a peak out = XQTL_zoom(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan, "chr3R", 13000000, 15000000)  # Then plot the region around the peak A1 = XQTL_region(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan, out$chr, out$start, out$stop, "Wald_log10p") A2 = XQTL_change_average(zinc_hanson_means, out$chr, out$start, out$stop) A3 = XQTL_genes(dm6.ncbiRefSeq.genes, out$chr, out$start, out$stop)

title: Using XQTL2.Xplore
text: data follow formats: QTL scan data: Columns chr, pos, Wald_log10p, optionally cM Frequency data: Columns chr, pos, TRT, REP, founder, freq Gene data: Columns chr, start, end, gene_name, strand, is_utr Variant data: Columns CHROM, POS, type, subtype, genotype columns founder

title: Using XQTL2.Xplore
text: NCBI RefSeq: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/refseq/ VCF Format Specification: https://samtools.github.io/hts-specs/VCFv4.2.pdf

title: XQTL2: A Complete Workflow for XQTL Analysis
text: vignette demonstrates complete workflow XQTL (Experimental Quantitative Trait Locus) analysis using XQTL2 package. ’ll walk typical analysis steps initial data exploration detailed peak examination using example datasets ZINC Hanson experiment.

title: XQTL2: A Complete Workflow for XQTL Analysis
text: First, let’s load XQTL2 package example datasets:
code:
library(XQTL2.Xplore)  # Load example datasets data(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan)  # QTL scan results data(zinc_hanson_means)       # Founder frequency data  # Load reference data for genes and variants data(dm6.ncbiRefSeq.genes)    # Gene annotations data(dm6.variants)           # Variant data  # Display basic information about the datasets cat("ZINC Hanson pseudoscan data dimensions:", dim(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan), "
") #> ZINC Hanson pseudoscan data dimensions: 25977 8 cat("ZINC Hanson means data dimensions:", dim(zinc_hanson_means), "
") #> ZINC Hanson means data dimensions: 3987264 6 cat("Available chromosomes:", unique(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan$chr), "
") #> Available chromosomes: chr2L chr2R chr3L chr3R chrX

title: XQTL2: A Complete Workflow for XQTL Analysis
text: first step QTL analysis visualize genome-wide association results. XQTL2 provides several Manhattan plot options:     Note: 5-panel Manhattan plots particularly useful high-quality datasets allow estimate peak locations within ~2Mb intervals. Genetic distance (cM) plots help identify broad peaks centromeric regions may actionable.
code: # 5-panel Manhattan plot with physical distance (Mb) - useful for precise peak localization XQTL_Manhattan_5panel(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan, cM = FALSE) #> Warning: Removed 5210 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_point()`). # 5-panel Manhattan plot with genetic distance (cM) - useful for broad peak identification XQTL_Manhattan_5panel(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan, cM = TRUE) #> Warning: Removed 5210 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_point()`). # Traditional Manhattan plot with physical distance XQTL_Manhattan(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan, cM = FALSE, color_scheme = "UCI") # Traditional Manhattan plot with genetic distance XQTL_Manhattan(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan, cM = TRUE)

title: XQTL2: A Complete Workflow for XQTL Analysis
text: ’ve identified regions interest, examine allele frequencies change treatment control conditions:
code: # Basic frequency change analysis XQTL_change_average(zinc_hanson_means, "chr3R", 18000000, 20000000) # Frequency change with reference strain highlighting XQTL_change_average(zinc_hanson_means, "chr3R", 18000000, 20000000, reference_strain = "A1") # Frequency changes by replicate XQTL_change_byRep(zinc_hanson_means, "chr3R", 18000000, 20000000)

title: XQTL2: A Complete Workflow for XQTL Analysis
text: XQTL_zoom function extremely productive tool refining peak boundaries. finds maximum association score region defines interval boundaries based specified drops significance:  Tip: Adjust left right drop parameters ’re satisfied peak boundaries. Smaller drops create narrower intervals, larger drops create broader intervals.
code: # Find and refine peak boundaries # Arguments: data, chromosome, start, stop, left_drop, right_drop out <- XQTL_zoom(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan, "chr3R", 18000000, 20000000, 3, 3)  # View the refined peak out$plot # Display the new interval boundaries cat("Refined interval:", out$chr, ":", out$start, "-", out$stop, "\n") #> Refined interval: chr3R : 18910000 - 18960000

title: XQTL2: A Complete Workflow for XQTL Analysis
text: refined peak boundaries, create detailed visualizations region:
code: # Create individual plots for the refined region A1 <- XQTL_region(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan, out$chr, out$start, out$stop, "Wald_log10p") A2 <- XQTL_change_average(zinc_hanson_means, out$chr, out$start, out$stop) A2b <- XQTL_change_average(zinc_hanson_means, out$chr, out$start, out$stop, plotSelection = TRUE) A3 <- XQTL_genes(dm6.ncbiRefSeq.genes, out$chr, out$start, out$stop) #> Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0. #> ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead. #> ℹ The deprecated feature was likely used in the XQTL2.Xplore package. #>   Please report the issue to the authors. #> This warning is displayed once per session. #> Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was #> generated. A4 <- XQTL_variantsByFounder(dm6.variants, out$chr, out$start, out$stop, zinc_hanson_means) A5 <- XQTL_SVBySize(dm6.variants, out$chr, out$start, out$stop, zinc_hanson_means)  # Combine plots using patchwork library(patchwork) A1 / A3 / A2 #> Warning: Removed 4 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_segment()`). A4 / A5

title: XQTL2: A Complete Workflow for XQTL Analysis
text: publication, create comprehensive 5-panel plot combines analyses:
code: # Create publication-ready 5-panel plot XQTL_5panel_plot(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan, zinc_hanson_means,                   dm6.variants, dm6.ncbiRefSeq.genes,                   out$chr, out$start, out$stop) #> Scale for x is already present. #> Adding another scale for x, which will replace the existing scale. #> Scale for x is already present. #> Adding another scale for x, which will replace the existing scale. #> Scale for x is already present. #> Adding another scale for x, which will replace the existing scale. #> Scale for x is already present. #> Adding another scale for x, which will replace the existing scale. #> Warning: Removed 4 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_segment()`).

title: XQTL2: A Complete Workflow for XQTL Analysis
text: Let’s examine another region demonstrate workflow:
code: # Explore a peak on chromosome 2L out2 <- XQTL_zoom(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan, "chr2L", 6000000, 7500000, 2, 2) out2$plot # Create multi-panel plot for this region myplot <- XQTL_5panel_plot(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan, zinc_hanson_means,                             dm6.variants, dm6.ncbiRefSeq.genes,                             out2$chr, out2$start, out2$stop) #> Scale for x is already present. #> Adding another scale for x, which will replace the existing scale. #> Scale for x is already present. #> Adding another scale for x, which will replace the existing scale. #> Scale for x is already present. #> Adding another scale for x, which will replace the existing scale. #> Scale for x is already present. #> Adding another scale for x, which will replace the existing scale.  # Save the plot (uncomment to save) # png("combined_plot_test.png", height = 10, width = 7, units = "in", res = 300) # print(myplot) # dev.off()

title: XQTL2: A Complete Workflow for XQTL Analysis
text: Start 5-panel Manhattan plots precise peak localization Use genetic distance (cM) identify broad, potentially non-actionable peaks XQTL_zoom friend - ’s often productive starting point detailed analysis Examine frequency changes understand biological relevance peaks Look genes variants refined intervals identify candidate causal variants Use 5-panel plot publication-ready figures

text: XQTL2.Xplore provides comprehensive tools : - Genome-wide QTL visualization Manhattan plots - Regional analysis peak refinement tools - Frequency change analysis across experimental conditions - Gene variant annotation visualization - Publication-ready multi-panel plots

title: Visualization Tools for XQTL Analysis
text: installation, test everything works:
code: # Load the package library(XQTL2.Xplore)  # Check available data data(package = "XQTL2.Xplore")  # Load and examine example data data(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan) data(zinc_hanson_means) data(dm6.ncbiRefSeq.genes) data(dm6.variants)  # Quick test - should work without errors head(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan)

title: Visualization Tools for XQTL Analysis
text: Quick Context: Example study X-QTL mapping multiparental Drosophila population identify genomic loci associated zinc toxicity resistance. identified 10 genes significant genotype--treatment effects, including pHCl-2, encodes zinc sensor protein. provides pathway broader understanding biological impact metal toxicity. Test core functionality: Expected output:  Description: 1. Top panel (Wald_log10p): - line graph showing statistically significant genetic association position along genomic region. y-axis shows Wald -log10(p-value), tests difference founder allele frequencies position — larger values (values 10) mean stronger evidence QTL location. - study, signal peaks around 32 near 18.935 Mb, exceptionally strong result indicating high-confidence evidence zinc resistance QTL region. Second panel (Frequency(Z-C): panel shows change frequency founder’s alleles zinc-selected control conditions. Positive values mean founder’s alleles increased frequency zinc selection (suggesting zinc resistance), negative values indicate decrease (suggesting zinc sensitivity). study, black line (founder A7) rises zero, meaning A7 alleles became common zinc stress appear confer protection. green line (founder A3) drops zero, indicating A3 alleles selected founder sensitive zinc. Third panel (Gene tracks): panel gene track showing exon structure candidate genes region. Blue rectangles represent exons genes transcribed left right (5’ 3’), orange rectangles represent genes reverse strand (3’ 5’). Within gene, thicker rectangles coding exons thinner ones 5’ 3’ UTRs. genes shown — Xrp1, Sgsh, Mpc1, EndoA, CG43732, CG42613 — sit underneath QTL peak therefore candidate contributors zinc resistance. exon structures shown help identify parts gene might disrupted nearby structural variants. Fourth panel (Structural Variants Founder): panel shows structural variants (SVs) carried 8 founders across region. horizontal colored line represents one founder, using color scheme Panel 2. Symbols along lines indicate individual SV events, shape color encoding SV type — upward triangles deletions, downward triangles insertions, red filled symbols indicate mobile element variants, . Triangle size constant reflect variant size. SV event assigned letter label. **Fifth panel (Structural Variants Scale): bottom track shows lettered SV events Panel 4, now drawn scale physically larger variants appear larger shapes. makes possible visually compare variant sizes across founders. large red arrows labeled E F near 18.930–18.938 Mb stand particularly large mobile element variants present founders A5, A7, AB8, making notable candidates functional investigation.
code: # Load the package and data library(XQTL2.Xplore) data(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan) data(zinc_hanson_means) data(dm6.ncbiRefSeq.genes) data(dm6.variants)  # Find a peak in a genomic region out <- XQTL_zoom(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan, "chr3R", 18000000, 20000000, 3, 3)  # View the zoomed region print(out$plot)  # Create a publication-ready 5-panel plot XQTL_5panel_plot(zinc_hanson_pseudoscan, zinc_hanson_means,                   dm6.variants, dm6.ncbiRefSeq.genes,                   out$chr, out$start, out$stop)                   #More zoomed in? XQTL_5panel_plot(df1, df2, dm6_variants, dm6_genes,                  chr = "chr2L",                  start = 18930000,                  stop  = 18940000)

what: Read and process multiple VCF files (SLOW - for data creation use create_variants_object.R instead) — DELETE_read_vcfs
title: Read and process multiple VCF files (SLOW - for data creation use create_variants_object.R instead) — DELETE_read_vcfs
text: Reads processes multiple VCF files containing different types genetic variants (SNPs, INDELs, SVs). function designed data creation computationally intensive. regular use, processed variants data object used instead. function converts VCF files tidy format standardized genotype coding variant classification.

title: Read and process multiple VCF files (SLOW - for data creation use create_variants_object.R instead) — DELETE_read_vcfs
code: DELETE_read_vcfs(vcf_paths, variant_types, variant_subtypes = NULL)

title: Read and process multiple VCF files (SLOW - for data creation use create_variants_object.R instead) — DELETE_read_vcfs
text: vcf_paths Character vector paths VCF files process variant_types Character vector specifying type VCF file (e.g., "SNP", "INDEL", "SV") variant_subtypes Character vector specifying subtype VCF file (e.g., "high", "moderate")

title: Read and process multiple VCF files (SLOW - for data creation use create_variants_object.R instead) — DELETE_read_vcfs
text: tidy data frame columns: CHROM, POS, type, subtype, recoded genotype columns founder

title: Read and process multiple VCF files (SLOW - for data creation use create_variants_object.R instead) — DELETE_read_vcfs
code: # Read multiple VCF files variants <- DELETE_read_vcfs(   vcf_paths = c("high_impact_snps.vcf", "moderate_impact_snps.vcf"),   variant_types = c("SNP", "SNP"),   variant_subtypes = c("high", "moderate") ) #> Processing high_impact_snps.vcf... #> Warning: cannot open file 'high_impact_snps.vcf': No such file or directory #> Error in file(file, "r"): cannot open the connection

what: Create a 5-panel XQTL plot with shared X-axis and combined legend using patchwork only — XQTL_5panel_plot
title: Create a 5-panel XQTL plot with shared X-axis and combined legend using patchwork only — XQTL_5panel_plot
text: Generates comprehensive 5-panel visualization combining QTL association results, frequency changes, structural variants, gene annotations genomic region. function creates publication-ready figure aligned X-axes across panels manually constructed combined legend showing founder colors structural variant types.

title: Create a 5-panel XQTL plot with shared X-axis and combined legend using patchwork only — XQTL_5panel_plot
code: XQTL_5panel_plot(   df1,   df2,   dm6_variants,   dm6_genes,   chr,   start,   stop,   reference_strain = NULL )

title: XQTL Manhattan Plot — XQTL_Manhattan
text: Creates Manhattan plot XQTL analysis results, showing association statistics across genome. function generates traditional Manhattan plot chromosomes arranged along x-axis -log10(p-values) y-axis. supports physical (Mb) genetic (cM) distance scales, includes multiple color schemes different institutions.

title: XQTL Manhattan Plot — XQTL_Manhattan
code: XQTL_Manhattan(df, cM = FALSE, color_scheme = "KU")

title: XQTL Manhattan Plot — XQTL_Manhattan
text: df data frame containing QTL scan results columns: chr, pos, Wald_log10p, optionally cM cM Logical, whether use genetic distance (cM) instead physical distance (Mb) x-axis color_scheme Character string specifying color scheme. Options include: "KU", "UCI", "Stanford", "Harvard", "MIT", "Berkeley", "McMaster", "McGill", "Oxford", "Cambridge", "NineInchNails"

title: XQTL Manhattan Plot — XQTL_Manhattan
text: ggplot object showing Manhattan plot association statistics

title: XQTL Manhattan 5-panel Plot — XQTL_Manhattan_5panel
text: Creates multi-panel Manhattan plot showing chromosome separately facet. function generates five individual Manhattan plots (one per chromosome) arranged vertically, making easier examine detailed patterns within chromosome. panel shows association statistics independent scaling better visualization chromosome-specific features. multiple_traits_column provided, points colored column using standard 8-color palette legend added. function uses tidyverse/dplyr idioms tidy evaluation throughout.

title: XQTL Manhattan 5-panel Plot — XQTL_Manhattan_5panel
code: XQTL_Manhattan_5panel(df, cM = FALSE, multiple_traits_column = NULL)

title: XQTL Manhattan 5-panel Plot — XQTL_Manhattan_5panel
text: df data frame containing QTL scan results columns: chr, pos, Wald_log10p, optionally cM cM Logical, whether use genetic distance (cM) instead physical distance (Mb) x-axis multiple_traits_column Optional. Character string specifying column df represents different studies/traits. provided, points colored column using standard 8-color palette legend added.

title: XQTL Manhattan 5-panel Plot — XQTL_Manhattan_5panel
text: ggplot object five faceted Manhattan plots, one per chromosome

what: Plot structural variants by size — XQTL_SVBySize
title: Plot structural variants by size — XQTL_SVBySize
text: Creates specialized visualization structural variants (SVs) showing relative sizes within genomic region. function displays SVs triangles heights proportional size, deletions shown center line insertions . Different SV types color-coded labeled easy identification.

title: Plot structural variants by size — XQTL_SVBySize
code: XQTL_SVBySize(   variants,   target_chr,   target_start,   target_stop,   df2,   reference_strain = NULL )

title: Plot structural variants by size — XQTL_SVBySize
text: variants Data frame containing variant information REF ALT columns size calculation target_chr Character string specifying chromosome analyze (e.g., "chr2L") target_start Integer specifying start position base pairs target_stop Integer specifying stop position base pairs df2 Data frame containing founder frequency data determine founder genotypes reference_strain Optional character string specifying reference strain (used plot)

title: Plot structural variants by size — XQTL_SVBySize
text: ggplot object showing structural variants sizes represented triangle heights

what: Plot average frequency changes or selection coefficients by position — XQTL_change_average
title: Plot average frequency changes or selection coefficients by position — XQTL_change_average
text: Creates line plot showing either frequency changes (Δq) selection coefficients (s) across genomic region. function visualizes allele frequencies change treatment control conditions, corresponding selection coefficients, founder strain across specified genomic interval.

title: Plot average frequency changes or selection coefficients by position — XQTL_change_average
code: XQTL_change_average(   df,   chr,   start,   stop,   reference_strain = NULL,   filter_low_freq_founders = TRUE,   plotSelection = FALSE )

title: Plot average frequency changes or selection coefficients by position — XQTL_change_average
text: df Data frame containing frequency data columns: chr, pos, founder, TRT, freq, REP chr Character string specifying chromosome analyze (e.g., "chr2L") start Integer specifying start position base pairs stop Integer specifying stop position base pairs reference_strain Optional character string specifying reference strain highlight grey filter_low_freq_founders Logical, whether apply transparency low-frequency founders plotSelection Logical, whether plot selection coefficients (s) instead frequency changes (Δq)

title: Plot average frequency changes or selection coefficients by position — XQTL_change_average
text: ggplot object showing frequency changes selection coefficients position

title: Plot average frequency changes or selection coefficients by position — XQTL_change_average
text: function handles low-frequency founders differently depending plot type: frequency change plots (plotSelection = FALSE): Founders average frequency < 2.5% across interval plotted reduced transparency (alpha = 0.2) low-frequency founders often change frequency much, want -emphasize selection coefficient plots (plotSelection = TRUE): Founders average frequency < 2.5% across interval completely excluded plot Founders positions frequency < 2.5% plotted reduced transparency (alpha = 0.1) filtering necessary normalization 2pq can produce large, potentially meaningless values s minor allele frequency small Low-alpha lines plotted background maintain visual hierarchy Selection coefficients calculated s = Δq/(2pq), Δq frequency change 2pq heterozygosity control condition (p=1-q). normalization can amplify noise p q small.

title: Plot a genomic region — XQTL_region
